items.title: School Exam Practice: Grade 12 Text Types

lessonBlocks.instructionId: Latihan ujian kelas 12: teks diskusi (dua sisi), teks aplikasi, dan tulisan untung-rugi.
lessonBlocks.originalContentNote: Seluruh soal adalah konten orisinal Nexus StoryArc.

lessonBlocks.textEn: SHOULD FINAL-YEAR STUDENTS TAKE PART-TIME JOBS? The question splits every staff room. Supporters point to three benefits. Work teaches time discipline no timetable can; a student who answers to a manager learns that deadlines are promises, not suggestions. Earnings also reduce family pressure, which for some students decides whether school continues at all. Finally, early work experience makes post-graduation applications concrete rather than theoretical. Opponents answer with equally real costs. Sleep and study time shrink first — teachers report that working students' grades dip most in the final semester, exactly when they matter most. Some employers exploit young workers with late shifts and vague contracts. And the "experience" gained is often repetitive labor with little learning value. A sensible middle path exists: limited hours (below fifteen per week), only in registered workplaces, and paused during exam months. The question is not whether students may work, but under what protections.

lessonBlocks.textEn: ARTICLE — COASTAL CITIES QUIETLY WINNING THE INTERNSHIP RACE. A new pattern is emerging in this year's placement data. Mid-size coastal cities like Tanjung Laras placed 34 percent more vocational interns than last year, while capital placements stayed flat. Three forces drive the shift. Tourism recovery doubled hotel and hospitality openings; the new ferry terminal is expected to add another wave next year. Second, remote work returned young professionals to their hometowns, and the studios they founded now take local interns. Third — the least discussed — smaller companies supervise better: intern-to-mentor ratios average three-to-one, against eleven-to-one in capital firms. The lesson for final-year students is unfashionable but practical: the biggest name on the building is not the biggest learning opportunity inside it. Read the mentor ratio before you read the logo.

lessonBlocks.transcriptEn: EXIT INTERVIEW (recording): SUPERVISOR: Twelve weeks done. Sit — this one's informal. What did the internship actually teach you? INTERN: Three things, honestly. First: confirming instructions isn't slowing people down — my worst day here came from a guess, my best days came from thirty-second confirmations. Second: reports work when they're honest; I stopped writing fairy tales in week four and my mentor started trusting me with bigger tasks the same week. Third — and nobody warned me about this one — endings matter. How you leave a shift, a complaint, a job… people remember the ending. SUPERVISOR: (laughs) We should record this for orientation. Oh — we did. That's what this recorder is for. INTERN: …That's why you said "informal", isn't it. SUPERVISOR: Welcome to management techniques. Final question: if a future intern asks about us, what will you say? INTERN: That the mentor ratio matters more than the building's logo. And that the coffee machine lies on Mondays.

lessonBlocks.textEn: LETTER TO NEXT YEAR'S FINAL-YEARS (school bulletin): You will receive, in the next twelve months, more advice than you can carry. Let me hand you a filter instead of more luggage. When someone says "obviously" about your future — obviously take the scholarship, obviously stay close to home — put the advice down and walk away; "obviously" is what people say when they haven't imagined being you. When two options tie on paper, the lists are not broken: you are weighing them without weights. Ask instead which regret is heavier at twenty-five, and trust the silence that follows — the answer lives there. And when you finally choose: choose loudly. Tell people. Plans announced grow roots; plans whispered stay wishes. If your first choice falls through — and some will — remember that a backup executed with full effort beats a dream attempted at half strength. We tested this. Repeatedly. Signed: three people who survived two letters, one scholarship, and a wedding bay full of suitcases.
